reason: Both compile and run with no timeouts, hallucinated APIs, or invented assets (agentTimedOut false, exit 0 on both). Prompt 02 is not a designated hard prompt (07/08/10), so hardPromptPass is false. Aura3D: emitter nozzle, glowing ring, and dark ground disc are clearly identifiable, and a working emission-rate slider plus a lifetime color key are present. However the prefabs.particleFountain + two effects.particles layers render as long, continuous orbital ribbon/streak loops rather than discrete gravity-affected particles tracing an upward-and-falling parabolic arc; lifetime color variation is barely perceptible (streaks read mostly white, with only faint yellow/blue specks), so two of the five required-evidence items are weakly satisfied. Three.js: a genuine point-sprite fountain with real gravity integration, ground-collision bounce, and a cyan->yellow->red lifetime color lerp; emitter (cone+base+ring), grid ground plane, controllable rate (240/sec, slider actually drives spawn count), upward-and-falling spray, and visible color variation are all present, matching the prompt's physics intent more faithfully despite a sparse spray at the captured low rate. Modularity favors Aura: its declarative scene() builder is more composable and concise, though rebuilding the entire app via dispose()+createAuraApp on every slider input is a notable design weakness; the Three.js code is imperative but cleanly factored into spawnParticle/updateParticleColor/animate helpers. The visual edge in prompt fidelity outweighs Aura's modularity edge, giving Three.js the overall win, though it is close. Note metric tradeoffs: Aura bundle is larger (208KB vs 120KB gz) and slower to first render (152.8s vs 143.2s). No screenshot/metrics contradiction observed; metrics only assert compile/run, which the screenshots corroborate.
